coredata.dc:title: Effective adsorption and sensitive detection of Cr6+ by degradable collagen-based porous fluorescent aerogel
coredata.dc:creator: Jing, Limin
Yang, Shuo
Li, Xia
Jiang, Yifei
Lou, Jiang
Liu, Zhuqing
Ding, Qijun
Han, Wenjia
coredata.dc:description: Although the treatment of heavy metal ions in environmental wastewater has attracted researchers' attention, it is still a great challenge to prepare green adsorbents with biodegradability, high adsorption efficiency, and high detection sensitivity. Herein, a bio-based, 3D network structured, luminescent aerogel with excellent adsorption efficiency and detection sensitivity for detecting and removing Cr6+ in an aqueous solution was demonstrated. The bifunctional aerogels were designed and synthesized using waste collagen, polyethyleneimine (PEI), and carbon dots cross-linked by aldehyde cellulose nanofibers. Sorption experiments were designed to investigate the sorption properties of Cr6+ through response surface methodology (RSM). The kinetic model (pseudo-second-order model) and isotherm model (Langmuir model) well-matched with the adsorption process. The maximum adsorption capacity by degradable collagen-based porous fluorescent aerogel for Cr6+ reached 103.3 mg.g−1. In addition, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent aerogel and the Cr6+ concentration. In addition, the adsorption mechanism, fluorescence properties, microstructure, and surface morphology have been thoroughly studied. The proposed bifunctional aerogels could be attractive for smart sorbents in the field of removal and detection of metal ions in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-based adsorbent
Collagen
Fluorescent aerogel
Cr6+
Cellulose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of adsorption and fluorescence sensing of Cr6+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of 3D network structure fluorescent aerogel.

(a) UV–vis spectra (red curve), picture of CDs solution irradiated by ultraviolet lamp shown in the inset. (b) PL excitation (green curve, λem = 444 nm), and PL emission (red curve, λex = 352 nm) spectra of CDs. (c) Emission spectra of CDs excited by different wavelengths. (d) AFM image of CDs. (e) Emission spectra of CDs at different pH. (f) Emission spectra of CDs in different metal ions. (g) AFM image of CNF. (h) AFM image of A-CNF. (i) FT-IR spectra of A-CNF.

(a), (b) SEM image of aerogels. (c) Pore size distribution of aerogel. (d) Effect of PEI content on aerogel qe (e) Effect of PEI addition on aerogel swelling rate. (f) The thermal stability of aerogel.

3D surface plots for interactions between (a, d) the volume of Cr6+ solution and temperature of solution, (b, e) initial pH and the volume of Cr6+ solution, (c, f) initial pH and temperature of solution.

(a) Initial Cr6+ concentration dependence of adsorption isotherms of aerogel. (b) Equilibrium isotherm was fitted by Langmuir model. (c) Equilibrium isotherm was fitted by Freundlich model. (d) Adsorptive removal. (e) Adsorption data were fitted using the pseudo- second-order model. (f) Adsorption data were fitted using the pseudo-first-order model.
The mechanism of the adsorption and fluorescence sensing of Cr6+.

(a) Wide-scan XPS spectra of adsorbed aerogel (b) XPS spectra of Cr6+ that was adsorbed onto aerogel (c) Comparison of the effects of metal ions using synthetic aerogels. (d) The fluorescence intensity of synthetic aerogels varies with the concentration of Cr6+ ions.
Thermodynamic parameters of aerogels adsorption for Cr6+.
The fitting parameters of adsorption kinetics of Cr6+.
Maximum adsorption capacities in comparison with previous work.

row.entry: Ball milling biochar iron oxide composites
Artemisia monospermic (AM) powder modified by tri-methyl octadecyl ammonium bromide

head.ce:title: Effective adsorption and sensitive detection of Cr6+ by degradable collagen-based porous fluorescent aerogel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Qilu University of Technology，Shandong Academy of Sciences, Jinan 250353, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology，Shandong Academy of Sciences, Jinan 250353, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Lab of Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Qilu University of Technology，Shandong Academy of Sciences, Jinan 250353, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education, Qilu University of Technology，Shandong Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although the treatment of heavy metal ions in environmental wastewater has attracted researchers' attention, it is still a great challenge to prepare green adsorbents with biodegradability, high adsorption efficiency, and high detection sensitivity. Herein, a bio-based, 3D network structured, luminescent aerogel with excellent adsorption efficiency and detection sensitivity for detecting and removing Cr6+ in an aqueous solution was demonstrated. The bifunctional aerogels were designed and synthesized using waste collagen, polyethyleneimine (PEI), and carbon dots cross-linked by aldehyde cellulose nanofibers. Sorption experiments were designed to investigate the sorption properties of Cr6+ through response surface methodology (RSM). The kinetic model (pseudo-second-order model) and isotherm model (Langmuir model) well-matched with the adsorption process. The maximum adsorption capacity by degradable collagen-based porous fluorescent aerogel for Cr6+ reached 103.3 mg.g−1. In addition, there was a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent aerogel and the Cr6+ concentration. In addition, the adsorption mechanism, fluorescence properties, microstructure, and surface morphology have been thoroughly studied. The proposed bifunctional aerogels could be attractive for smart sorbents in the field of removal and detection of metal ions in wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: The removal of heavy metals from industrial wastewater was one of the important research contents in the field of environmental protection (Albadarin et al., 2012). Entering the environment, heavy metals always remain, migrate, accumulate, and can hardly be biodegradable (M. et al., 2016). Through the food chain, they must be threaten ecological security and human health. Cr6+ is one of the priority toxic pollutants in many industries, such as tanning, mining, metallurgy, and so on (Bilal et al., 2013). Moreover, chromium ions can lead to serious diseases such as diarrhea, lung cancer, renal failure, and so on. The treatment of chromium pollution has attracted great attention of researchers. It is a very important research topic to design and prepare efficient adsorbents to remove chromium ions.

So far, the common treatment methods for chromium pollution are chemical precipitation, flotation, ion exchange solvent extraction, membrane separation, and adsorption. Among them, adsorption has attracted more attention and application because of its simple operation and low cost (Barrera-Diaz et al., 2012). Common environmental friendliness, renewability and extensive biological adsorbents, such as tannin, cellulose (Choi et al., 2020), hemicellulose, and lignin (Jiang et al., 2019), have been widely studied. The cellulose-based composite membrane prepared by Zhou et al. using cellulose nanofibers (CNF) and functional carboxyl groups in bovine serum albumin as model proteins showed good protein adsorption performance, with an adsorption capacity of 241.6 mg.g−1 (Zhou et al., 2019). Wu et al. studied the adsorption of Cr3+ by black liquor before lignin separation in order to evaluate the possibility of using lignin to remove Cr3+ from water. The maximum adsorption capacity of Cr3+ had reached 17.97 mg.g−1 (Wu et al., 2008). Wei et al. prepared two carbon materials as adsorbents for Cr6+ by green hydrothermal method from hemicellulose. After hydrothermal treatment, the hemicellulose was spherical, and the maximum adsorption capacity was 74.60 mg.g−1 (Wei et al., 2021). Collagen fibers (CF) extracted from waste leather shavings are rich in -COOH, -NH3, -OH, and other active groups, and have been used for Cr6+ adsorption through chelation (Ahn et al., 2021; Ghazanfari et al., 2016; Li et al., 2008). Although the adsorption capacity of CF for heavy metal ions is weak, It can be optimized by modification of functional groups or compounding with other materials (Wang et al., 2013). Himarati Mondal et al. introduced CF into apple pomace pectin (APPN)-graft-pentamer and prepared a functional hybrid biocomposite adsorbent with maximum adsorption capacities of 171.79 and 177.27 mg.g−1 for Ti4+ and As5+ (Mondal et al., 2021). Compared with other bio-based adsorbents, collagen fiber-based adsorbents come from industrial waste, have obvious cost advantages, simple modification method and low energy consumption. To date, bio-based adsorbents extensively studied only have a single adsorption function. Producing a dual-functional bio-based adsorbent with high adsorption capacity and semi-quantitative detection remains a challenge. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) is rich in amino groups, which can provide more reactive sites for chelating metal ions (Li et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2020; Lv et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018). Cellulose nanofibers (CNF) can not only provide a natural framework to assemble a 3D network structure (Weissl et al., 2019), but also provide a large number of surface hydroxyl groups to promote the accumulation and adsorption of heavy metal ions (Pei et al., 2021; Rao et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2017). Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have broad application prospects in the field of fluorescence sensing and can be used as a suitable fluorescence source for synthetic aerogels due to their high thermal stability (Rimal et al., 2020), good biocompatibility (Tungare et al., 2020), and high fluorescence quantum yield (Xie et al., 2019).

Here, we designed and prepared fluorescent aerogels for adsorption and detection of Cr6+ by waste collagen fibers, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and carbon dots cross-linked by aldehyde cellulose nanofibers(A-CNF). This prepared aerogel can realize sensitive fluorescence sensing and high-efficiency adsorption. The optimal experimental conditions were determined by response surface methodology and its adsorption behavior was studied in detail, including adsorption mechanism, fluorescence properties, microstructure and surface morphology. The prepared dual-function smart aerogel has great application potential in the field of removing and detecting metal ions in wastewater.

Collagen fiber (CF) and cellulose nanofibers (CNF) were obtained from Shengquan Group Co., Ltd (Jinan, China). Polyethyleneimine (50 wt%, 50% in water, PEI), NaIO4 (AR grade) and citric acid (AR grade) sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR grade) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical. Ethylenediamine (AR grade) was supply from Damao Group Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). All reagents in this work were directly obtained through procurement without further purification.

Citric acid（0.84 g）and ethylenediamine (1.072 ml) were mixed and dissolved in 20 ml ultrapure water under stirring. The obtained mixture was placed in a stainless-steel autoclave lined with 25 ml Teflon and reacted at 180 °C for 5 h. After the reaction, the product was cooled to room temperature and poured into a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight = 10,000) for purification. After dialyzed with fresh water every 8 h for 48 h, the resulting solution was poured into a dialysis bag (retaining molecular weight = 1000) and dialyzed until the pH reaches neutral.

CNF（0.12 g）and NaIO4 (0.24 g) was added to the brown bottle and reacted for 18 h without light. Then, 20 ml of ethylene glycol was added to terminate this oxidation reaction. The resulting solution was poured into the dialysis bag (retained molecular weight = 1000) and dialyzed until the pH value reached neutral. The obtained product was stored at low temperature for standby. The content of the aldehyde group was determined by the hydroxylamine hydrochloride method.

A series of aerogels were synthesized by simple Schiff base reaction. Initially, PEI and A-CNF were diluted 20 times and 3 times, respectively, and stirred continuously at room temperature for standby. Then, the diluted PEI, CDs and CF were completely mixed. Dilute A-CNF was added at room temperature for free radical polymerization. Finally, the products of fluorescent aerogels were obtained by freeze-drying.

The fluorescence properties of the prepared CDs and fluorescent aerogels were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer and steady-state, transient fluorescence spectrometer (F-7100, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) in the wavenumbers of 4000–400 cm−1 was used to characterize the synthesized CDs and A-CNF. The morphology of the CDs, CNF before and after aldehyde were observed by an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Brooke, Multimode8, USA). The thermal stability and composition of aerogels were studied by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA-50). Under the nitrogen flow (40 ml.min−1), the temperature of 10 mg sample increased from 25 °C at the rate of 10 °C min−1, and the change of weight loss (%) with temperature between 45 and 600 °C was recorded. The specific surface areas were measured by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method based on N2 adsorption. The concentrate ion of Cr6+was measured by an atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS) (GGX-600, China).

Cr6+-based adsorption experiments were carried out on the prepared aerogels. Five parallel experiments were performed in the adsorption experiments and mean values and error bars were obtained. The equilibrium adsorption capacity qe can be calculated by the following formula: The qe (equilibrium adsorption capacity) can be calculated by the following equation.
(1)
q
e
=
C
0
−
C
e
× V
m

The qt (adsorption capacity at time t) was calculated by the following formula:
(2)
q
t
=
C
t
−
C
e
× V
C
0

In the formula, C0 and Ce (mg.L−1) were the concentration of the initial solution and equilibrium solution, respectively. m (g) is the mass of the aerogel sample. v (L) is the volume of the fractional Cr6+ solution. Ct was the concentration of Cr6+ in the solution at time t.

The adsorption percentage of Chromium ions was calculated as follows:
(3)
η =
C
0
−
C
e
C
0
× 100 %

Adsorption isotherm experiment: Firstly, 120 ml Cr6+ solutions of different initial concentrations (20, 50, 100 mg. L–1) were put into a 250 ml conical flask. Secondly, for each flask, one synthesized aerogel sample (around 100 mg) was added. Finally, all reaction systems were placed in a constant temperature shaking water bath for 48 h, the solution in each flask was filtered with an aqueous filter membrane (0.22 µm), and the concentration was measured to calculate the corresponding qe.

Adsorption kinetics experiment: Initially, 120 ml Cr6+ solution with fixed concentration (50 mg.L−1) was put into a 250 ml conical flask and a synthetic aerogel sample (about 100 mg) was added to each flask. Then, all conical flasks was put into a constant temperature shake flask bath (65 °C) for a series of equilibrium times (6, 12, 30, 60, 66, 72, 90, 120, 160 min, etc.). After the solution was filtered using the filter membrane (0.22 μ m), the concentration of the solution was measured in each conical flask.
After adsorbing Cr6+ on fluorescent aerogels, the metal ions were removed by NaOH solution under ultrasonic condition, and then regenerated three times to obtain regenerated aerogels.

As shown in Fig. 1, the synthetic route of fluorescent aerogels with a 3D network structure is environmentally friendly due to the non-toxic and renewable nature of the raw materials. Firstly, CDs were synthesized from ethylenediamine and citric acid by the one-step hydrothermal method. Secondly, A-CNF as a green cross-linking agent was obtained by the oxidation of CNF by NaIO4. CF and PEI were the skeletons of fluorescent aerogels, and CF came from industrial wastes in the leather industry. Finally, aerogels as target products were obtained by additional polymerization. As a fluorescent sensor, CDs can not only detect Cr6 + concentration but also induce Cr6 + aggregation. Due to these unique structures of aerogels, they can be used as effective adsorbents and detection materials.

In the UV absorption spectra of CDs, two absorption bands with centered at 240 nm and 344 nm were observed, as shown in Fig. 2a. At the same time, the illustration showed that the prepared CDs emitted blue fluorescence. Fig. 2b showed PL excitation spectra and PL emission spectra. The excitation wavelength of CDs ranges from 200 to 410 nm, and the maximum excitation occurs at 365 nm (Fig. 2b, green line). Under 352 nm excitation, the PL spectrum of CDs solution was centered at 444 nm (Fig. 2b, red line). Under 352 nm excitation, the absolute quantum yield of CDs was 52.02%. Fig. 2c showed the emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths. The results showed that the position of the emission peak moved slightly in a wide excitation wavelength range. That is, the sample showed a slight excitation-dependent emission. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 392 nm excitation wavelength can reached about 8400. The emission behavior of synthetic fluorescence aerogels was based on excitation. This behavior was essentially due to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carbonyl, which contained many local energy levels (Li et al., 2017b). FT-IR analysis showed that the appearance of stretching vibrations of N − H was at 3389 cm−1, and C−H was at 2939 cm−1, and bending vibrations of N − H was at 1576 cm−1 (Fig. S1). The appearance of these peaks indicated the successful modification of amino groups on the surface of CDs. The AFM analysis images of the CDs illustrate their reasonable monodispersity (Fig. 2d). From the image we can see that CDs have a nano spherical structure with a uniform particle size. The fluorescence intensity of CDs at different pH was shown in Fig. 2e. The fluorescence intensity was high and the fluorescence stability was good in the range of pH 4 ~ 8. This result was presumed to be the result of changes in the surface properties and electron leaps of fluorescent molecules (Sheng et al., 2020). The change of pH value will only reduce the emission intensity of the sample but will not affect the wavelength (Fig. 2e), which is consistent with the previous report (Liang et al., 2013). The specificity of the synthesized CDs for different heavy metal ions is shown in Fig. 2f. Compared with the low effect on the fluorescence behavior of Ba2+, Fe3+ and Cu2+, Cr6+ exhibited the most pronounced fluorescence burst. This may be because Cr6+ has the strongest electron capture ability (Xue et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2013). As a result, more electrons can be transferred from the fluorescent molecule to Cr6+ , leading to the clearest fluorescence burst signal.

Fig. 2g, h showed the AFM images of cellulose before and after aldehyde modification. Their heights range from − 3.3–5.8 nm and − 2.0–3.7 nm. It can be seen from the images that the modification does not change the nano size of cellulose. FT-IR analysis showed that the appearance of stretching vibrations of CO was at 1728 cm−1 (Fig. 2i). The content of the aldehyde group was 11.2% calculated by the hydroxylamine hydrochloride method. The elemental analysis of CF and PEI was shown in Table S1. The addition of PEI can effectively increase the amino content. The addition amount of A-CNF was determined by the sum of nitrogen elements.

The morphological and structural characterizations of the synthesized fluorescent aerogel were studied via SEM. Fig. 3a, b showed the morphological of the dry saturated aerogels. It can be clearly found that the network structure has pores ranging from 1 µm to 20 µm. The average pore size and specific surface area of fluorescent aerogels are shown in Table S2. Fig. 3c showed that the pore size distribution of aerogel was mostly concentrated between 20 and 40 nm, and the mesoporous materials led to a larger specific surface area, which was conducive to the diffusion of adsorbates on the surface of the adsorbent (Bu et al., 2013). The amount of PEI added to the gel was determined by its influence on the aerogel qe and swelling rate. The qe of aerogels was relatively high when PEI was added to 50% as shown in Fig. 3d. Fig. 3e showed that the swelling rate of aerogels decreased following the increase of the amount of addition. This phenomenon was attributed to the acetyl group as a hydrophobic group in PEI (Zhao et al., 2015). The stress-strain curve showed that the aerogel had good mechanical strength (Fig. S2). Meanwhile, the thermal degradation temperature of aerogel was above 150 °C and had good thermal stability, which could meet the adsorption temperature of heavy metal removal from wastewater (Fig. 3 f).

The effect of initial adsorbate solution volume on the equilibrium absorptive capacity of synthetic fluorescent aerogels is shown in Fig. 4. Under the condition of the same mass of adsorbent, the equilibrium adsorption capacity and removal rate increase with the increase of the initial adsorbate volume. The increase of adsorbate ion concentration improves the electrostatic attraction between adsorbate and adsorbent and promotes the redox reaction (Nasef and Yahaya, 2009). In the subsequent experiments, the initial volume of adsorbate solution was fixed at 120 ml.

As shown in Fig. 4 and Fig S3, the adsorption capacity of aerogels increases with increasing pH. The adsorbent has a higher adsorption capacity and removal rate when pH = 6 and then decreases as the pH increases. The surface charge property of adsorbent and the existence form of adsorbate were affected by the pH value of solution system. As shown in Fig. 4 and Fig. S3, By designing 2, 4, 6, 8 different pH response surface experiments, t the adsorption capacity of aerogels increased with the increase of pH, the adsorbent had high adsorption capacity and removal rate at pH = 6, and then decreased with the increase of pH. The ionization degree of the amino group is different under different pH conditions. It is easy to protonate under near weak acidic condition, aerogels has high reaction activity with metal ions and has high adsorption capacity (Xia et al., 2018). It can be concluded that water pollution can be easily solved by the new synthesized aerogel and has good stability in a wide pH range. In this study, pH = 6 was used as the most appropriate pH value in the follow-up test.

By designing a series of response surface experiments with different temperatures, the results show that the equilibrium adsorption capacity and removal rate of aerogels increase with the increase of temperature. As shown in Fig. 4, the experimental results can be explained by the change of Gibbs free energy. As shown in Table 1, the positive enthalpy change implies that the reaction is endothermic. The increase in temperature is conducive to the reaction. The reaction was conducted at 52 °C for the subsequent adsorption process by optimization of experimental conditions.

The initial concentration had a good linear relationship with the equilibrium adsorption capacity between 20 and 150 ppm ( Fig. 5a). The equilibrium capacity of the aerogel increased with the raise of initial concentration of Cr6+. Meanwhile, Fig. 5b, c showed the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherms of this aerogel. The fitting parameters R2 of the two models were calculated. The values of the Freundlich model were slightly larger than those of the Langmuir model, but the difference was not significant. Therefore, the synthetic fluorescent aerogels in the adsorption process can be represented by the Freundlich model. The time-dependent adsorption isotherms of the aerogel were showed in Fig. 5d. As shown in Fig. 5d, the average adsorption equilibrium time was about 4–5 h. The adsorption data of aerogels were fitted by pseudo-second-order model and pseudo-first-order model in Fig. 5e, f and Fig. S4 respectively. Obviously, in Table 2, the correlation coefficient (R2) of the pseudo-second-order model was more than that of the pseudo-first-order model, and the adsorption kinetics of the synthesized fluorescent aerogel could be better described by the pseudo-second-order model, which indicated that the adsorption process of heavy metals by aerogels included the common results of physical adsorption and chemisorption. Intra-particle diffusion belongs to the second stage of the diffusion process. The constant C deviates from the origin indicating that external and internal diffusion of ions dominate the whole adsorption process (Zhang et al., 2021) (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of adsorption and fluorescence sensing of Cr6+
ce:section.ce:para: When the synthetic aerogel is immersed in a solution of Cr6+, The Cr6+ diffuses to the surface of the synthetic aerogel owing to the electrostatic action of the positively charged amino group. The prepared aerogel skeleton can be used as the adsorbent and collector of heavy metal ions, which can accelerate the diffusion rate of metal ions (Li et al., 2017a). The channels for diffusion of metal ions from the surface to the interior were provided by the 3D network structure of the aerogel. Hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in collagen fibers also coordinate with metal ions. As shown in Fig. 7a,b, four peaks at 577.4, 589.3, 576.4 and 585.4 eV appeared, which were due to Cr6+, N-Cr, Cr3+ of Cr 2p (1/2) and Cr 2p (3/2), respectively. Cr6+ was reduced to Cr3+, which effectively reduced the harm of Cr6+ to the ecosystem. Cr6+ and Cr3+ can also be enriched on adsorbents by chelation. Therefore, the adsorbent can effectively remove Cr6+ from water. The above "adsorption coupled reduction" process is widely believed to be the mechanism of Cr6+ adsorption, and the elemental SEM mapping of Cr6+ further confirmed this phenomenon (Fig. S6). The reusability of fluorescent aerogels was shown in Fig. S7. With the increase of cycle times, the adsorption capacity decreases gradually, which was mainly due to the strong coordination ability between amino and metal ions.

More importantly, CDs with high quantum yield are fixed in the 3D network structure, which can enhance the stability of fluorescence signal (Yuan et al., 2020). When Cr6+ ions are diffused into the synthetic fluorescent aerogel, they can be adsorbed to CDs due to the induction of the amino group. The synthetic fluorescent aerogel showed a rapid visual response ( Fig. 6). Meanwhile, Fig. 7a shows obvious color changes of metal ion solution before and after adsorption. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescent aerogel has a good adsorption selectivity for Cr6+. This may be because Cr6+ has the strongest electron capture ability. As shown in Table 3, the synthesized fluorescent aerogels are also significantly larger adsorption of Cr6+ than other bio-aerogels previously reported. At the same time, because the fluorescence intensity has a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9922) with the initial concentration of the metal solution, we can semi-quantitatively detect the concentration of the solution (Fig. 7,d). In summary, the aerogel could also be used as a sensitive optical sensor for Cr6+.

In summary, we demonstrated a biological-based, 3D network structure, luminescence and aerogel with excellent adsorption efficiency and detection sensitivity for the detection and removal of Cr6+. Fluorescent aerogels are designed by using collagen, polyethyleneimine (PEI) and CDs to cross-link aldehyde cellulose nanofibers. The adsorption process conforms to the pseudo-second-order kinetic model and Langmuir model. The maximum adsorption capacity of the synthesized fluorescent aerogel for Cr6+ reached 103.3 mg. g−1, and there was a good linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Cr6+ concentration. This material could potentially be used for a wide range of applications in the field of removing and detecting metal ions in wastewater.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research Program of China (2020YFC1910301). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2021QC158). Innovation Pilot Project of Integration of Science, Education and Industry of Shandong Province (2020KJC-ZD06). Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Biobased Material and Green Papermaking, Qilu University of Technology, Shandong Academy of Sciences (No. ZZ20200107, No. ZZ20200118 & ZZ20190111). National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22001140). Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2020QB002). Outstanding Youth Innovation Team Project of Shandong Provincial University (242007040109).

ce:section.ce:para: Wenjia Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Loujiang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yifei Jiang: Visualization, Investigation. Qijun Ding and Xia Li: Supervision. Zhuqing Liu: Software, Validation. Limin Jing and Shuo Yang: Writing – review & editing. All authors had drafted, revised the work and approved the final paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2022.114882.
